CSCE 222 Syllabus

Section 504 (61763)

Discrete Structures for Computing

Fall 2025 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 3:55PM

End Time: 5:10PM

End Date: 12/16/2025

Meeting Location:  EABB 106

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Timothy Davis

Email:  davis@tamu.edu

Office:  235 Peterson

Phone:  979 845 4094

Office Hours

10am-11am, Tuesdays and Thursdays

Preferred Contact Method

email, text, or cell phone (cell: 352 359 2812)

Biography

Tim Davis is a Professor of Computer Science at Texas A&M University. His primary

scholarly contribution is the creation of widely-used sparse matrix algorithms and

software (SuiteSparse), including most of the sparse solvers behind x=A\b in

MATLAB, and many other commercial packages. Fellow IEEE, SIAM, ACM.

Course Description

CSCE 222 Discrete Structures for Computing. Credits 3. (3 Lecture Hours).

Mathematical foundations from discrete mathematics for analyzing computer

algorithms, for both correctness and performance; introduction to models of

computation, including finite state machines and Turing machines.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147,

MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross Listing: ECEN 222/CSCE 222.

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Discrete Mathematics is to computer science as words and grammar are to English.

Solving a problem in computer science without discrete math is like writing a novel

with garbled grammar or a confusing plot. It just won't work. Your thinking will be

muddled, and your code just won't work. Worse yet, your code will likely have

hidden bugs that won't be found until an end-user complains (or explodes ...). At

that point, fixing the bug is costly, or even impossible.

This course teaches you the fundamentals of logic, proof techniques,

induction/recursion, counting, advanced counting (not as easy as it sounds!),

relations, graph theory, models of computing, and finite state machines. These

mathematical tools are essential to doing and understanding computer science /

computer engineering.

Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

ISBN:  1260920240

Authors:  K. Rosen

Publisher:  McGraw-Hill

mathematical tools are essential to doing and understanding computer science /

computer engineering.

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications

ISBN:  1260920240

Authors:  K. Rosen

Publisher:  McGraw-Hill

Publication Date:  2025

Edition:  2025 Release

Notes:

You must use the 2025 Release (in any format: ebook, print, etc).  You cannot use

the 8th (or earlier) editions.  See:

https://www.mheducation.com/highered/product/discrete-mathematics-and-its-

applications-rosen.html

Grading Policy

Exams 75%: we will have two in-class exams during the semester, and a final exam.

Each is worth 25% each.

Homework: 25%. Homework is always due at EXACTLY 3:55pm on a class day, so

that I can discuss the solution in class that day. So normally I cannot accept late

submissions, except with prior permission. Homework is assigned, submitted, and

graded on Canvas. Unless otherwise noted on the assignment, all homework is to

be done by yourself. You may discuss the homework with each other but you must

solve the problems yourself. When discussing with each other, pretend you are in

my office discussing it with me. I will help you understand the problem but I

wouldn't give you the solution. If you do discuss the homework with anyone, state

their name on your submission (either on the HW itself or on a note on Canvas).

You may not use AI in any way.

Grading scale (after curving of individual items, as needed):

90 or higher: A

80: B

70: C

60: D

less than 60: F

The University views class attendance as the responsibility of an individual student.

Attendance is essential to complete the course successfully. University rules related

to excused and unexcused absences are located on-line at  http://student-

rules.tamu.edu/rule07 ; In general, I do not give excused absences for interviews or

travel, but exceptions are granted on a case-by-case basis. You must ask me

beforehand, however. If you miss class because of medical reasons or for an

interview (for work or for graduate / professional school) that cannot be

rescheduled, I reserve the right to require written confirmation. For interviews or

other scheduled events, a priori confirmation is required.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

No late assignments accepted without a priori permission.  Turning in a homework

assignment one minute past the due date

Topics, with sections/chapters of the book:

1 The Foundations: Logic and Proofs

1.1 Propositional Logic

1.2 Applications of Propositional Logic

1.3 Propositional Equivalences

1.4 Predicates and Quantifiers

1.5 Nested Quantifiers

1.6 Rules of Inference

1.7 Introduction to Proofs

1.8 Proof Methods and Strategy

2 Basic Structures: Sets, Functions, Sequences, Sums, and Matrices.

2.1 Sets

2.2 Set Operations

2.3 Functions

2.4 Sequences and Summations

2.6 Matrices

3 Algorithms

3.1 Algorithms

3.2 The Growth of Functions

3.3 Complexity of Algorithms

4 Number theory and cryptography

4.1 Divisibility and Modular Arithmetic

4.3 Primes and Greatest Common Divisor

5 Induction and Recursion

5.1 Mathematical Induction

5.2 Strong Induction and Well-Ordering

5.3 Recursive Definitions and Structural Induction

5.4 Recursive Algorithms

6 Counting

6.1 The Basics of Counting

6.2 The Pigeonhole Principle

6.3 Permutations and Combinations

8 Advanced Counting Techniques

8.1 Applications of Recurrence Relations

8.3 Divide-and-Conquer Algorithms and Recurrence Relations

9 Relations

9.1 Relations and Their Properties

9.3 Representing Relations

9.5 Equivalence Relations

10 Graphs (may be truncated depending on time)

10.1 Graphs and Graph Models

10.2 Graph Terminology and Special Types of Graphs

10.3 Representing Graphs

10.4 Connectivity

11 Trees (as time permits)

11.1 Introduction to Trees

11.2 Applications of Trees

11.3 Tree Traversal

13 Modeling Computation

13.2 Finite-State Machines with Output

13.3 Finite-State Machines with No Output

13.4 Language Recognition

13.5 Turing Machines

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance is expected but not logged.

AI Statement

No AI is permitted in any manner.
